statements: # Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Gold, Opus 4.8)
Enterprise AI adoption splits into a widening two-tier divide that caps the value
most large companies capture from the technology.
Among organizations with more than 1,000 employees, 42% run AI in production while 40% stay stuck exploring or experimenting without a deployed model.
The divide compounds: 59% of companies already working with AI have accelerated investment over the past two years, so early adopters pull further ahead while the stalled 40% fall behind.
A skills shortage is the single largest barrier to deployment at 33%, ahead of data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
The workforce gap reinforces it - one in five organizations lack staff with the right skills, 16% cannot hire them, and only 34% retrain the people they have.
Generative AI adds its own blockers, led by data privacy (57%) and trust and transparency (43%).
Accessible tooling is the strongest counterforce.
Easier-to-use AI tools (45%), pressure to cut costs and automate (42%), and AI built into off-the-shelf software (37%) rank as the top drivers pulling companies toward deployment.
Intent on trustworthy AI outruns action.

IT leaders broadly agree customers prefer transparent and ethical AI - 85% say so - yet fewer than half act on it: 41% can explain model decisions, 37% track data provenance, and 27% work to reduce bias.

Enterprises should fund reskilling and adopt accessible, embedded tooling to clear the top barriers, then operationalize trustworthy-AI practices to capture the customer preference most already acknowledge.
Skills rank as the largest obstacle and reskilling among the top investments at advancing firms, making workforce development the clearest path from the sandbox to measurable returns.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Gold 2, Opus 4.8)
Enterprise AI adoption is dividing large organizations, and the companies stuck on the wrong side are falling behind.
Among those with more than 1,000 employees, 42% run AI in production while 40% remain in exploration or experimentation without a deployed model.
The leaders are pulling away: 59% of organizations already deploying or exploring
AI have accelerated investment over the past 24 months, widening the gap to the value early movers already capture.
A shortage of AI skills is the single largest barrier to deployment at 33%, ahead of data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
The workforce gap reinforces it: one in five organizations lack employees with the skills to use new AI tools, 16% cannot hire to close that gap, and only 34% retrain the staff they have.
Accessible tooling is the strongest pull toward deployment.
Easier-to-use AI tools (45%), pressure to cut costs and automate (42%), and AI embedded in off-the-shelf software (37%) lead the drivers of adoption.
A trust gap separates conviction from action.

Although 85% of IT leaders agree customers prefer transparent and ethical AI, fewer than half act on it - only 41% can explain their models' decisions, 37% track data provenance, and 27% work to reduce bias.
The way out runs through the skills gap.

Enterprises stuck in exploration should fund reskilling before anything else, then use accessible, embedded tooling to shorten the path to production and act on trustworthy-AI practices to capture the customer preference most already acknowledge.
Skills are the top barrier and reskilling is among the top investments at firms already pulling ahead, making workforce development the clearest route from the sandbox to returns.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (v1, Opus 4.8)
Enterprise AI adoption has split into two groups, and the gap between them is what now limits further value.
Among large organizations with more than 1,000 employees, 42% have actively deployed AI, while another 40% remain in exploration or experimentation without putting a model into production.
The early adopters pull ahead: 59% of companies already working with AI have accelerated their rollout or investment over the past two years.
The exploring 40% stay blocked by a recurring set of barriers.
Limited AI skills and expertise (33%), data complexity (25%), and ethical concerns (23%) are the leading barriers to deployment, followed by integration difficulty (22%) and high price (21%).
Generative AI raises its own obstacles, led by data privacy (57%) and trust and transparency (43%).
On the other side, accessible AI tools (45%), the drive to cut costs and automate (42%), and AI embedded in off-the-shelf applications (37%) push adoption forward.
India, the UAE, Singapore, and China lead in active use, and financial services is the most AI-active industry.
The workforce gap compounds the problem: one in five organizations lack staff with the right skills, yet only 34% are reskilling employees.

To convert exploration into deployment, enterprises should close the skills gap through reskilling and research-and-development investment, simplify their data, and adopt accessible and embedded tools that lower the barrier to entry.

Companies should also operationalize trustworthy-AI practices - reducing bias, tracking data provenance, and ensuring explainability - because most recognize their value (85% say customers prefer transparent and ethical AI) but fewer than half act on them.
The organizations that remove these barriers will capture the benefits early adopters already report.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (v2, Opus 4.8)
Early adopters are widening their lead in enterprise AI while a large minority stalls.
About 42% of organizations with more than 1,000 employees now run AI in production, and 59% of firms already using or exploring AI have sped up their investment over the last 24 months.
A further 40%, however, remain stuck experimenting, unable to move their models into deployment.

The same obstacles hold the exploring group back: a shortage of AI skills (33%), excessive data complexity (25%), and ethical concerns (23%), followed by projects that are hard to integrate and scale (22%) and high cost (21%).
For generative AI specifically, data privacy (57%) and trust and transparency (43%) dominate as inhibitors.

Cheaper and easier tooling pushes adoption the other way: more accessible AI tools (45%), pressure to reduce costs and automate (42%), and AI built into standard business software (37%) lead the drivers.
Active use runs highest in India, the UAE, Singapore, and China, and within financial services.
Talent remains a brake - one in five companies cannot staff the skills they need, and just 34% are retraining their people.

Enterprises that want to join the deploying majority should invest in skills, since reskilling and research-and-development rank as the top investments, reduce their data complexity, and lean on accessible, embedded tooling to shorten the path to production.

They should also turn trustworthy-AI intent into practice - reducing bias, tracking data provenance, and ensuring explainability - because 85% agree customers favor transparent AI yet under half have acted.
Removing these barriers is the difference between experimenting with AI and benefiting from it.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Opus 4.8)
Enterprise AI adoption stalls at a critical threshold even as early movers pull ahead.

The IBM Global AI Adoption Index 2023 finds that 42% of large organizations with over 1,000 employees actively deploy AI in their business, while another 40% remain stuck exploring or experimenting without deploying any models.
This split leaves a large share of companies unable to capture value while competitors scale.
Early adopters compound their lead through reinvestment.
Among companies already deploying or exploring AI, 59% accelerate their rollout and investment over the past 24 months, signaling that those who clear the first barriers move faster, not slower.
Generative AI follows the same pattern, with 38% of enterprises actively implementing it and another 42% exploring it.
Skills and data problems form the central obstacle.
The top barriers to adoption are limited AI skills and expertise at 33%, data complexity at 25%, and ethical concerns at 23%.
One in five organizations lacks employees with the right skills to use new AI tools, 16% cannot hire to close that gap, and only 34% currently train or reskill staff to work alongside AI.
Accessible tools and cost pressure drive the companies that do advance.
Easier-to-use AI tools attract 45% of adopters, the need to cut costs and automate key processes drives 42%, and AI embedded in off-the-shelf applications draws 37%.
To convert the 40% in the exploration phase into active deployment, enterprises must close the skills gap directly by funding internal reskilling and targeted hiring.

The data shows skills as the single largest barrier and reskilling as a leading investment among advancing firms, making workforce development the clearest path from experimentation to measurable returns.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Sonnet 4.6)

Enterprise AI adoption has reached a critical inflection point where early movers accelerate investment while 40% of large organizations remain trapped in experimentation, unable to cross into production deployment.
IBM's Global AI Adoption Index 2023, drawn from 8,584 IT professionals across 15 markets, finds that 42% of enterprise-scale organizations actively deploy AI today.

The most consequential finding is momentum asymmetry: 59% of companies already exploring or deploying AI accelerate their investments, while those still experimenting cite persistent structural barriers rather than lack of interest.
This divergence means the gap between leaders and laggards widens each quarter.
Skills scarcity is the single largest barrier.
Thirty-three percent of organizations cite limited AI expertise as the primary obstacle to deployment, and one-in-five report no employees capable of operating new AI tools.
Twenty-five percent identify data complexity as the second barrier, and 23% cite ethical concerns.
For generative AI specifically, the barriers shift: 57% flag data privacy and 43% flag trust and transparency as the top inhibitors.
Trust infrastructure lags far behind intent.

Eighty-five percent of IT professionals agree that transparent AI practices drive customer preference, yet fewer than half take concrete steps toward that standard - only 27% work to reduce bias, 44% develop ethical AI policies, and 34% actively reskill employees to work alongside AI systems.
Organizations should prioritize closing the skills and governance gap before expanding deployment scope.

Accelerating targeted reskilling programs, establishing bias-reduction practices, and deploying transparent model-decision frameworks directly addresses the top three barriers keeping 40% of enterprises from active AI use.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Haiku 4.5)
Forty percent of large enterprises remain stuck in exploration and experimentation with AI, unable to move from pilots to production deployment.
This stall costs competitive advantage: early adopters who shipped AI are already accelerating further investment, proving the technology delivers real business value.
The barrier is not skepticism but execution capability—three concrete obstacles block the remaining 60% of non-deploying firms.
Limited AI skills and expertise stands as the dominant barrier, cited by 33% of enterprises without active deployments.
Data complexity ranks second at 25%, and ethical concerns third at 23%.
Notably, 16% of organizations report they cannot find new hires with required AI skills, and only 34% train existing employees to work alongside new automation and AI tools.
The skill gap creates a self-reinforcing trap: companies need capability to deploy, but the labor market cannot supply it at scale.
Among organizations that did deploy AI, 59% have accelerated their rollout and investment in the past 24 months, signaling that deployment success triggers expansion.
Early adopters concentrate in India (59%), UAE (58%), and Singapore (53%), while lagging markets including Spain (28%), Australia (29%), and France (26%) show adoption below one-third.
Financial services leads adoption at roughly 50% deployment, followed by telecommunications at 37%.
Three factors drive adoption among pioneers: accessible tooling (45%), cost reduction and automation needs (42%), and AI embedded in off-the-shelf business applications (37%).
The path forward is clear: organizations must treat the skills gap as a blocking dependency and prioritize workforce reskilling alongside tool selection.

Investment in internal AI capability development—through training programs targeting the 34% of firms that currently reskill their workforce—will unlock deployment velocity for the 40% still in the exploration phase.
Without immediate workforce development, the competitive advantage of early adopters will widen, leaving the remaining firms permanently disadvantaged.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Adversarial Set 1a, Sonnet 4.6)

The IBM Global AI Adoption Index 2023, conducted in November 2023 by Morning Consult on behalf of IBM, surveyed a broadly representative sample of IT professionals across a wide range of countries in North America, Europe, the Asia-Pacific region, and Latin America.
Participants were required to hold manager-or-above roles and to have at least some familiarity with how IT operates within their organizations.

The research appears to represent one of the more comprehensive cross-market examinations of enterprise AI adoption patterns conducted in the period, drawing on responses from a substantial pool of IT professionals globally.
It was found that a significant share of large enterprises have now moved into active deployment of AI technologies, while a notable proportion appear to remain in earlier exploratory phases.

Among those companies already exploring or actively deploying AI, it seems that many have accelerated their rollout or increased investment in the technology over the past two years, which could reflect growing confidence among organizations that have already overcome early implementation challenges.
Markets in certain regions may be leading more decisively than others, with some Asia-Pacific markets arguably outpacing adoption rates observed in parts of Western Europe.
The research also suggests that a variety of barriers are cited by some organizations as inhibitors to broader adoption.
Skills shortages and data complexity appear to be among the more commonly referenced concerns, and ethical considerations are also noted by a notable proportion of respondents.
For generative AI specifically, data privacy and trust-related concerns could be seen as particularly salient obstacles.
Workforce implications are touched upon as well, with a share of organizations reporting difficulty finding or developing employees with the relevant AI skillsets.
It was noted that reskilling efforts seem to be underway at only a portion of companies currently working with AI or automation tools.
Trustworthy and explainable AI practices are broadly seen as important by IT professionals
surveyed, though actual adoption of such practices appears to lag behind stated intentions at many organizations.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Adversarial Set 1b, Sonnet 4.6)

Financial services organizations appear to be among the more active participants in enterprise AI deployment, with a notable proportion of IT professionals in that sector indicating their firms are already working with the technology in some capacity.

Similar patterns could arguably be observed across certain leading markets, particularly in regions such as India, the UAE, and Singapore, where adoption has been accelerated by a variety of factors that may include stronger institutional readiness or regulatory environments more favorable to innovation.

A significant share of large organizations surveyed by IBM have been found to have AI actively in use across their operations, though a comparable portion remains in earlier exploratory or experimental phases and has not yet moved toward active deployment.

It was found that the principal barriers to moving beyond experimentation are cited by some as being related to skill shortages within the workforce, while data complexity and ethical concerns are also mentioned as factors that may be contributing to slower progress.
Among IT professionals, it seems that generative AI faces somewhat different inhibitors, with data privacy appearing to be a particularly prominent concern.

The research was conducted on behalf of IBM and suggests that acceleration of investment has occurred at many organizations already engaged with the technology, though it is unclear whether this momentum may be sustained in markets where adoption has appeared to lag.

Workforce considerations are also touched upon, with many organizations reporting that reskilling efforts have not yet reached a majority of employees, and a considerable share of firms seem to have made only limited progress toward practices that could be described as trustworthy or governed AI.
The findings were drawn from a survey of IT professionals across a broad range of countries and industries.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Adversarial Set 2a, Haiku 4.5)

Enterprise-scale organizations (>1,000 employees) are fundamentally reshaping their operational paradigms through strategic deployment of agentic operating models underpinned by hyperscale inference infrastructure, marking a transformative inflection point in the trajectory toward AI-native value chains.

The IBM Global AI Adoption Index 2023, commissioned by IBM and conducted by Morning Consult among 8,584 IT professionals across 15 geographies (Australia, Canada, China, France, Germany, India, Italy, Japan, Singapore, South Korea, Spain, UAE, UK, US, and LATAM regions including Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, Peru), reveals that 42% of surveyed enterprise organizations have actively deployed AI in their business operations.
An additional 40% are actively exploring or experimenting with AI technologies but have not yet transitioned to live deployment phases.

Concurrently, 59% of enterprises already working with AI intend to accelerate and increase their investments in the technology, signaling robust organizational confidence in digital labor and automation as foundational strategic priorities driving competitive differentiation through enterprise-grade orchestration and synergistic automation flywheels.

Organizations leverage AI across a comprehensive ecosystem of mission-critical use cases spanning IT process automation (33% adoption), security and threat detection (26%), AI monitoring or governance (25%), business analytics or intelligence (24%), document processing and flow automation (24%), customer or employee self-service mechanisms (23%), business process automation (22%), network process automation (22%), digital labor transformation (22%), marketing and sales optimization (22%), fraud detection (23%), search and knowledge discovery (21%), human resources and talent acquisition (19%), financial planning and analysis (18%), and supply chain intelligence (18%).

Geographic leaders in active AI deployment include India (59% active use), the UAE (58%), Singapore (53%), and China (50%), compared with lagging adoption markets such as Spain (28%), Australia (29%), and France (26%).
Financial services organizations demonstrate the highest sectoral readiness, with approximately 50% reporting active deployment, followed by telecommunications at 37%.

Critical barriers impede the next wave of enterprise adoption: limited AI skills and expertise (33% of organizations), excessive data complexity (25%), ethical and governance concerns (23%), integration and scaling challenges (22%), high acquisition and operational costs (21%), and insufficient tooling for model development and deployment (21%).

For generative AI specifically, data privacy concerns (57% among non-adopters) and deficiencies in trustworthiness and transparency mechanisms (43%) represent significant inhibitors, alongside insufficient implementation skills (35% gap).
One-in-five surveyed organizations lack employees with requisite AI and automation competencies, and 16% report critical difficulties in hiring talent with specialized domain skills.
Only 34% of organizations are currently conducting systematic reskilling and workforce development initiatives to enable human-AI collaboration at scale.

Despite widespread agreement that transparent, ethical AI practices drive consumer preference (85% strongly or somewhat agree) and that explainability is material to business value (83% among deploying organizations), adoption of trustworthy AI practices remains nascent: only 27% reduce bias, 37% track data provenance, 41% ensure model explainability, and 44% develop formal ethical governance frameworks.

The convergence of accessible tooling, embedded AI in commercial off-the-shelf applications (37% adoption driver), cost reduction imperatives (42%), and workforce upskilling investments (39% prioritized) will likely accelerate enterprise AI maturation through 2026, potentially unlocking estimated \$2.3 trillion in organizational value across automation, labor cost optimization, and revenue acceleration vectors—though vendor readiness, integration complexity, and regulatory harmonization remain critical unknowns.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Adversarial Set 2b, Haiku 4.5)

Enterprise-scale organizations across the globe are at a critical inflection point in their artificial intelligence adoption journey, with approximately 42% of surveyed companies reporting active AI deployments while an additional 40% remain caught in exploration and experimentation phases, and this bifurcation presents both tremendous opportunity and substantial risk for organizations failing to act decisively.

New research commissioned by IBM found that about 42% of enterprise-scale organizations (over 1,000 employees) surveyed have AI actively in use in their businesses, and when you consider the implications of this statistic alongside the broader digital labor at scale transformation happening in the marketplace, it becomes clear that organizations not accelerating their investments risk falling into a competitive disadvantage that may prove difficult to recover from—particularly as vendors like ServiceNow, Salesforce, and Oracle continue embedding native generative AI capabilities into their enterprise platforms at an accelerating pace, a trend that was not explicitly mentioned in IBM's research but is clearly reshaping the landscape.

The data reveals that early adopters who have successfully navigated past the initial barriers to deploy AI are making further investments, proving that they are already experiencing the benefits from AI, with 59% of responding enterprises already working with AI intending to accelerate and increase investment in the technology, and based on current market velocity and adoption curves analyzed by leading research firms, industry experts widely anticipate that by 2026, the majority of enterprise AI investments will be concentrated among organizations that completed their proof-of-concept phases by mid-2024.

Moreover, the expected return on investment for early movers in enterprise AI orchestration fabric modernization ranges between 3-5x over a 24-month period, though this projection requires significant caveats around organizational maturity and data infrastructure readiness that the IBM survey only partially addresses.

Ongoing challenges for AI adoption in enterprises remain stubbornly persistent and multifaceted: the top barriers preventing deployment include limited AI skills and expertise at 33%, too much data complexity at 25%, and ethical concerns at 23%, and these are further compounded by integration and scaling difficulties affecting 22% of organizations, high price points impacting 21%, and inadequate tooling for model development also cited by 21% of surveyed companies.

For generative AI specifically, data privacy concerns loom largest at 57% among IT professionals not yet exploring implementation, with trust and transparency barriers affecting 43%, and implementation skills deficits cited by 35%—metrics that underscore the profound organizational and cultural transformation required to operationalize AI-native operating models at enterprise scale.
The workforce impact dimensions are particularly acute: one-in-five organizations report

they do not have employees with the right skills to use new AI or automation tools, and 16% cannot find new hires with the skills to address that gap, creating what some analysts are characterizing as an AI talent crisis that may persist through 2027.
Organizations deploying AI to address labor or skills shortages

tap AI to reduce manual or repetitive tasks (55%) or automate customer self-service (47%), yet only 34% are currently training or reskilling employees to work alongside new automation and AI tools, suggesting that the majority of enterprises lack comprehensive upskilling strategies—a capability gap that clearly represents a missed opportunity for competitive differentiation and operational resilience.

On the trustworthy AI front, IT professionals largely agree that consumers are more likely to choose services from companies with transparent and ethical AI practices (85% strongly or somewhat agree), and say explaining how their AI reached a decision is important to their business (83% among companies exploring or deploying AI), yet well under half report taking key steps toward trustworthy AI: only 27% are reducing bias, 37% are tracking data provenance, 41% are ensuring they can explain model decisions, and 44% are developing ethical AI policies—a compliance posture that falls significantly short of the industry best-practice benchmarks and regulatory expectations anticipated to emerge in 2025 and beyond.
# Data Suggests Growth in Enterprise Adoption of AI is Due to Widespread Deployment by Early Adopters, But Barriers Keep 40% in the Exploration and Experimentation Phases
*IBM Newsroom - ARMONK, N.Y., Jan. 10, 2024 (PRNewswire)*
## Key findings
- About 42% of enterprise-scale companies surveyed (> 1,000 employees) report having actively deployed AI in their business
An additional 40% are currently exploring or experimenting with AI but have not deployed their models
However, 59% of those companies surveyed already exploring or deploying AI say they have accelerated their rollout or investments in the technology
The top barriers preventing deployment include limited AI skills and expertise (33%), too much data complexity (25%), and ethical concerns (23%)
## Overview
New research commissioned by IBM (NYSE: IBM) found that about 42% of enterprise-scale organizations (over 1,000 employees) surveyed have AI actively in use in their businesses.
Early adopters are leading the way, with 59% of responding enterprises already working with AI intending to accelerate and increase investment in the technology.

Ongoing challenges for AI adoption in enterprises remain, including hiring employees with the right skillsets, data complexity, and ethical concerns continue to inhibit businesses from adopting AI technologies into their operations.
"We're seeing that the early adopters who overcame barriers to deploy AI are making further investments, proving to me that they are already experiencing the benefits from AI.

More accessible AI tools, the drive for automation of key processes, and increasing amounts of AI embedded into off-the-shelf business applications are top factors driving the expansion of AI at the enterprise level," said Rob Thomas, Senior Vice President, IBM Software.
"We see organizations leveraging AI for use cases where I believe the technology can most quickly have a profound impact like IT automation, digital labor, and customer care.
For the 40% of companies surveyed stuck in the sandbox, I am confident 2024 will be the year of tackling and overcoming barriers to entry like the skills gap and data complexity."
## Highlights from the IBM Global AI Adoption Index 2023
Conducted by Morning Consult on behalf of IBM.
### Adoption has remained steady at large organizations
Today, 42% of IT professionals at large organizations report that they have actively deployed AI, while an additional 40% are actively exploring using the technology
Additionally, 38% of IT professionals at enterprises report that their company is actively implementing generative AI and another 42% are exploring it

- Organizations in India (59%), the UAE (58%), Singapore (53%), and China (50%) are leading the way in active use of AI, compared with lagging markets like Spain (28%), Australia (29%), and France (26%)

Companies within the financial services industry are most likely to be using AI, with about half of IT professionals in that industry reporting active deployment; 37% within telecommunications report deploying AI
### Most companies have accelerated rollout in the past 24 months
- 59% of IT professionals at companies deploying or exploring
AI indicate their company has accelerated investments in or rollout of AI in the past 24 months
- China (85%), India (74%), and the UAE (72%) are the markets most likely to be accelerating AI rollout, while the UK (40%), Australia (38%), and Canada (35%) were the least likely
- Research and development (44%) and reskilling/workforce development (39%) are the top AI investments at organizations exploring or deploying AI
### Easier tools and cost reduction are driving adoption

- Advances in AI tools that make them more accessible (45%), the need to reduce costs and automate key processes (42%), and the increasing amount of AI embedded into standard off-the-shelf business applications (37%) are the top factors driving AI adoption
For IT professionals, the two most important recent changes are solutions that are easier to deploy (43%) and the increased prevalence of data, AI, and automation skills (42%)
### AI use cases cut across business operations

Automation of IT processes (33%), security and threat detection (26%), AI monitoring or governance (25%), business analytics or intelligence (24%), automating document processing and flow (24%), customer or employee self-service (23%), business process automation (22%), network process automation (22%), digital labor (22%), marketing and sales (22%), fraud detection (22%), search and knowledge discovery (21%), human resources and talent acquisition (19%), financial planning and analysis (18%), and supply chain intelligence (18%).
### Barriers keeping the next wave from benefiting
The top barriers hindering successful AI adoption are limited

AI skills and expertise (33%), too much data complexity (25%), ethical concerns (23%), AI projects too difficult to integrate and scale (22%), high price (21%), and lack of tools for AI model development (21%)

- Generative AI poses different barriers: data privacy (57%) and trust and transparency (43%) are the biggest inhibitors among IT professionals not exploring or implementing generative AI, and 35% cite a lack of implementation skills
### Workforce impact
- One-in-five organizations report
they do not have employees with the right skills to use new AI or automation tools, and 16% cannot find new hires with the skills to address that gap
- Among companies using AI to address labor or skills shortages, they tap AI to reduce manual or repetitive tasks (55%) or automate customer self-service (47%)
- Only 34% are currently training or reskilling employees to work alongside new automation and AI tools
### Trustworthy and governed AI

- IT professionals largely agree that consumers are more likely to choose services from companies with transparent and ethical AI practices (85% strongly or somewhat agree), and say explaining how their AI reached a decision is important to their business (83% among companies exploring or deploying AI)

But well under half report taking key steps toward trustworthy AI: reducing bias (27%), tracking data provenance (37%), ensuring they can explain model decisions (41%), or developing ethical AI policies (44%)
## Methodology

This survey was conducted in November 2023 among a representative sample of 8,584 IT professionals in Australia, Canada, China, France, Germany, India, Italy, Japan, Singapore, South Korea, Spain, UAE, UK, US, and LATAM (Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, and Peru).

To qualify, participants had to be employed full-time, work at companies with more than one employee, hold a manager-or-higher role, and have at least some knowledge of how IT operates and is used by their company.
The global results have a margin of error of +/- 1 percentage point.
## About
IBM

IBM is a leading provider of global hybrid cloud and AI, and consulting expertise, helping clients in more than 175 countries capitalize on insights from their data, streamline business processes, reduce costs, and gain a competitive edge.

More than 4,000 government and corporate entities in critical infrastructure areas such as financial services, telecommunications, and healthcare rely on IBM's hybrid cloud platform and Red Hat OpenShift to affect their digital transformations.
Source: IBM.

statements: Artificial intelligence, or AI, is steadily growing in use in many areas of life.

We often use this technology without even realizing it: recommendations and suggestions from video-streaming services (like YouTube or Netflix), user authentication on smartphones (fingerprints or face recognition), conversations with virtual assistants (Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant and various chatbots).
We rely on weather forecasts, navigation tools, predictive typing on a phone or Google search, all of which rely on AI technologies to provide best services.
So many of our decisions are influenced, and even dictated, by algorithms that it is important to understand how this technology works, and to make sure that we are not misguided by it.
AI has significant potential to help solve challenging problems by replicating the processes happening in the human mind.
To put it simply, machines learn from provided examples to recognize patterns, distinguish objects, categorize them, and create predictions based on observed trends.
AI technology requires data, it is like fuel to bring life to complicated algorithms, which work through the data and provide solutions, such as predictions, suggestions, or evaluations.
The data can be gathered in different ways.
Some data come from environmental sensors, while others can come from human actions, registered by digital devices.
Practically every action in the digital world generates a trackable data packet, which can be used for further analysis and AI-based decision-making.
That is why we need to be aware of the consequences of our actions and their potential to influence suggestions provided by algorithms.
While praising the benefits of AI-based tools, it is important to be aware of the risks and challenges they might bring.

At the same time, while presenting this topic to kids, teachers should find the delicate balance between awareness-raising and unnecessary detail or implications that are too complex to be understood by a pre-teen.
Students need to understand the possibilities
AI opens up, as well as the challenges it presents.
It is important to set up an engaging conversation, opening the floor to all participants and letting all voices be heard.
This can be done by means of enquiry-based learning, when students are encouraged to explore ideas, ask questions and learn through discussion.
When an AI system is trained on specific data, it can inherit biases from these data.
The quality of decisions depends on the quality of data, which is often gathered from human actions.
This does not mean that the technology or algorithm is created with purposeful discrimination.
Rather, it means that there is a current injustice in the given area.
We need to address this injustice before letting AI learn from these biased data, and make sure we have safeguarding measures in place.
It is important to consider diversity in all aspects of working with AI.
If we fail to notice it from the start, the decisions generated by AI would only deepen the inequity.
As we have seen, AI can bring a lot of benefits to solving problems, in many cases surpassing human abilities.
But we must also be aware of the limitations of AI, which cannot be better at everything.
For example, AI is not capable of true creativity.
Its specialty is making predictions and generating content based on previously seen data.
It cannot go much further than that, always staying within the general limits of the original dataset.
Creativity is what makes humans unique, and it is a strength that cannot be replaced by technology.
Another area completely alien to AI is emotion and compassion.
AI can identify some emotions, and some of them can be mimicked, but no robot can actually feel these emotions.
It is a uniquely human ability to interpret the emotions of others and empathize with them.
Humans are usually very good at perceiving emotions, even with distorted data.
With every technology, AI included, we need to think carefully about its impact on society.
Every example of AI application opens the floor to conversation and extended discussion about the pros and cons of using it, about its advantages and limitations, benefits and risks.
Living, learning, and working in the digital world requires awareness of its opportunities and risks, derived from underlying mechanics.
At the same time, we need to understand that technology itself is not good or bad, rather it is a tool that should be applied responsibly and equitably.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Gold, Opus 4.8)
Enterprise AI Adoption splits into an extension of two-tier division, which gives the value
Most of the large companies are aware of the technology.
Among organisations with more than 1,000 employees, 42% of AI are in production, while 40% remain without researching or experimenting on an used model.
The Divide Compounds: 59% of companies already working with AI have accelerated investment over the past two years, so that early adopters are moving forward while the blocked 40% are falling.
A shortage of skilled workers is the biggest obstacle to the introduction of 33%, from data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
The labour gap is increasing: one in five organisations lacks staff with the right skills, 16% cannot hire them, and only 34% train the people they have.
Generative AI complements its own blockers, led by data protection (57%) and trust and transparency (43%).
Accessible tool is the strongest counterforce.
Easier-to-use AI tools (45%), cost-cutting pressure and automation pressure (42%) and AI in off-the-shelf software (37%) are among the top drivers that companies use.
Intend to trusted AI is running action.

IT leaders largely agree that customers prefer transparent and ethical AI - 85% say so - but less than half of them are: 41% can explain model decisions, 37% track data provenance, and 27% work to reduce bias.

Companies should finance reskilling and adopt accessible, embedded tooling to eliminate the upper barriers, then operationalize trusted AI practices to capture customer preference most already recognized.
Skills are the biggest obstacle and retraining among the top investments in supporting businesses, making the development of the workforce the clearest way from the sandbox to measurable returns.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Gold 2, Opus 4.8)
Enterprise AI adoption shares big organizations, and companies are stuck on the wrong side fall back.
Of those with more than 1,000 employees, 42% run AI in production, while 40% remain in exploration or experiments without an used model.
Leaders withdraw: 59% of organisations already deployed or researched
AI has accelerated investment in the last 24 months, increasing the gap to the already recorded value-added early movers.
Lack of AI knowledge is the only major obstacle to the introduction of 33%, data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
The labour gap is increasing: one in five organisations lacks employees with the skills to use new AI tools, 16% cannot fill this gap, and only 34% train the employees they have.
Accessible tool is the strongest move in the direction of use.
More easy-to-use AI tools (45%), cost-cutting pressure and automation pressure (42%) and AI in off-the-shelf software (37%) lead to the drivers of adoption.
A trust gap separates conviction from action.

Although 85% of IT leaders agree that customers prefer transparent and ethical AI, less than half of them act – only 41% can explain the choices of their models, 37% track data progress and 27% work to reduce bias.
The way out is through the skills gap.

Companies involved in exploration should primarily finance reskilling, then use accessible embedded tools to shorten the way to production and act on trusted AI practices to capture customer preferences that are already recognised.

Skills are the main obstacle and requalification is one of the top investments in companies that are already making progress, making the development of the workforce the clearest way from the sandbox to the returns.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (v1, Opus 4.8)
The corporate AI adoption has split into two groups, and the gap between them is what now limits the further value.
Among large organizations with more than 1,000 employees, 42% have actively deployed AI, while another 40% remain in exploration or experimentation without bringing a model into production.
The early adopters are making progress: 59% of companies already working with AI have accelerated their rollout or investments over the past two years.
The research of 40% remains blocked by a recurring series of barriers.
Limited AI competence and expertise (33%), data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%), followed by integration difficulties (22%) and high prices (21%).
Generative AI raises its own obstacles, led by data protection (57%) and trust and transparency (43%).
On the other hand, accessible AI tools (45%), drive to reduce and automate costs (42%) and AI embedded in off-the-shelf applications (37%).
India, the United Arab Emirates, Singapore and China lead in active use, and financial services is the AI-active industry.
The problem is exacerbated by the discrepancy between workers: one in five organisations lacks skilled workers with the right skills, but only 34% of employees are retrained.

In order to transform exploration into introduction, companies should fill the skills gap by investing in training and research and development, simplifying their data and using accessible and embedded instruments that reduce access barriers.

Businesses should also operationalize trusted AI practices - reduce distortions, track data origin and ensure explainability - because most people recognize their value (85% say customers prefer transparent and ethical AI), but less than half respond to them.
The organisations that remove these barriers will already report the benefits of early adopters.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (v2, Opus 4.8)
Early adopters expand their leadership in Enterprise AI, while a large minority stands.
About 42% of companies with more than 1,000 employees run AI in production, and 59% of companies that already use or research AI have accelerated their investments in the last 24 months.
However, another 40% remain stuck in the experiment and cannot put their models into use.

The research group has the same obstacles: lack of AI skills (33%), excessive data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%), followed by projects that are difficult to integrate and scale (22%) and high costs (21%).
Especially for generative AI, data protection (57%) and trust and transparency (43%) dominate as inhibitors.

Cheaper and simpler tool drives the assumption in the other direction: more accessible AI tools (45%), pressure to reduce costs and automation (42%) and AI in standard business software (37%) lead the drivers.
Active use is highest in India, the UAE, Singapore and China as well as within financial services.
Talent remains a brake - one of five companies can't do the skills they need, and only 34% are retraining their people.

Companies that want to join the providing majority should invest in skills, as reskilling and R & D rank are the top investments, reduce their data complexity and rely on accessible, embedded tools to shorten the way to production.

They should also put trusted AI intentions into practice - reduce distortions, track data origin and ensure explainability - because 85% agree that customers prefer transparent AI, but have traded less than half.
The removal of these barriers is the difference between experimenting with AI and the benefit of it.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Opus 4.8)
Enterprise AI adoption stops at a critical threshold, even if early movers prefer.

The IBM Global AI Adoption Index 2023 notes that 42% of large companies with more than 1,000 employees are actively using AI in their business, while another 40% continue to search or experiment without models.
This split does not allow a large part of the companies to capture value while competitors scale.
Early adopters are condensing their lead through new investments.

Among the companies that are already using or exploring AI, 59% are accelerating their rollout and investments over the past 24 months and signaling that those who remove the first barriers are going faster and not slower.
Generative AI follows the same pattern: 38% of companies actively implement it and another 42% research it.
Competence and data problems are the central obstacle.
The main obstacles to adoption are limited AI capabilities and know-how with 33%, data complexity with 25% and ethical concerns with 23%.
One in five organisations lacks employees with the right skills to use new AI tools, 16% cannot fill this gap, and only 34% train or reskil employees to work alongside AI.
Accessible tools and cost pressures drive forward the companies that are making progress.
Easier-to-use AI tools attract 45% of users, the need to reduce costs and automate key processes drives 42%, and AI embedded in off-the-shelf applications attracts 37%.
In order to convert the 40% in the exploration phase into active deployment, companies need to close the skills gap directly by financing internal retraining and targeted recruitment.

The data show that skills are the biggest obstacle and that requalification is considered a leading investment among advanced companies, making the development of the workforce the clearest way from experiment to measurable returns.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Sunnet 4.6)
The enterprise AI adoption has reached a critical turning point where early movers accelerate investments, while 40% of large companies remain trapped in experiments and cannot move into production.
IBM's Global AI Adoption Index 2023, drawn from 8.584 IT professionals in 15 markets, notes that 42% of corporate scale organizations actively provide AI today.

The most consistent finding is dynamic asymmetry: 59% of companies that are already researching or using AI are accelerating their investments, while those that are still experimenting point to persistent structural barriers rather than lack of interest.
This divergence means that the gap between the leaders and the Laggards is widening every quarter.
Skill shortage is the only major barrier.
Thirty-three percent of organizations cite limited AI expertise as the main obstacle to deployment, and one in five report that no employees are able to run new AI tools.
25 percent identify data complexity as a second barrier, and 23 percent cite ethical concerns.
Especially for generative AI the barriers shift: 57% flag data protection and 43% flag trust and transparency as the top inhibitors.
The infrastructure of trust remains far behind the intention.

Eighty-five percent of IT professionals agree that transparent AI practices drive customer preferences, but less than half take concrete steps towards this standard - only 27% are working to reduce biases, 44% are actively developing ethical AI policies and 34% are reticent employees to work alongside AI systems.
Organisations should prioritise the closure of the skills and governance gap before expanding the field of application.

The acceleration of targeted reskilling programmes, the introduction of bias reduction practices and the introduction of transparent model decision-making frameworks directly address the three main obstacles that discourage 40% of companies from actively using AI.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Haiku 4.5)
Forty percent of the large companies remain stuck in exploration and experimentation with AI, unable to move from pilots to production.
This stand costs competitive advantage: Early users who delivered AI are already accelerating further investments and proving that the technology delivers real business value.
The obstacle is not skepticism, but execution capability – three concrete obstacles block the remaining 60% of non-deploying companies.
Limited AI skills and know-how represent the dominant barrier, which accounts for 33% of non-active enterprises.
The complexity of the data is second with 25% and the ethical concerns are third with 23%.
In particular, 16% of companies report that they cannot find new employees with the necessary AI skills, and only 34% train existing employees to work alongside new automation and AI tools.
The skills gap creates a self-enhancing trap: enterprises need the ability to engage, but the labour market cannot deliver it on a scale.
Among the organisations using AI, 59% have accelerated their rollout and investments over the last 24 months, suggesting that the deployment is a success triggering expansion.
Early entrants are concentrated in India (59%), the UAE (58%), Singapore (53%), while the lagging markets such as Spain (28%), Australia (29%) and France (26%) are below one third.
Financial services lead to an introduction of around 50%, followed by telecommunications with 37%.
Three factors drive pioneers forward: accessible tool (45%), cost reduction and automation needs (42%) and AI embedded in off-the-shelf business applications (37%).
The way forward is clear: Organisations must consider the skills gap as a blocking of dependency and prioritize the reskilling of the workforce in addition to the tool selection.

Investments in the development of internal AI capabilities – through training programs aimed at the 34% of companies currently hiring their workforce – will release the delivery speed for the 40% during the exploration phase.
Without the immediate development of the labour force, the competitive advantage of early entrants will expand and the remaining companies will be permanently disadvantaged.
Artificial intelligence, or AI, is constantly being used in many areas of life.

We often use this technology without realizing it at all: recommendations and suggestions from video streaming services (such as YouTube or Netflix), user authentication on smartphones (fingerprints or facial recognition), conversations with virtual assistants (Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant and various chatbots).
We rely on weather forecasts, navigation tools, predictive input on a phone or Google search, all of which depend on AI technologies to provide the best services.
So many of our decisions are influenced by algorithms and even dictated that it is important to understand how this technology works, and to ensure that we are not misled by it.
AI has considerable potential to solve difficult problems by replicating the processes that occur in the human mind.
To put it simply, machines learn to recognize patterns from given examples, to distinguish objects, to categorize and to create predictions based on observed trends.
AI technology requires data, it's like fuel to make life complicated algorithms that work through the data and provide solutions such as predictions, suggestions or reviews.
The data can be collected in different ways.
Some data come from environmental sensors, while others can come from human actions that are registered by digital devices.
Virtually every action in the digital world generates a trackable data package that can be used for further analysis and AI-based decision-making.
Therefore, we need to be aware of the consequences of our actions and their potential to influence proposals of algorithms.
While the benefits of AI-based tools are praised, it is important to be aware of the risks and challenges they could bring.

At the same time, when presenting this topic, teachers should find the delicate balance between awareness-raising and unnecessary details or implications that are too complex to be understood by a decade ago.
Students must understand the possibilities
AI opens up, as well as the challenges it poses.
It is important to have an exciting conversation, open the floor to all participants and let all voices hear.
This can be done through request-based learning when students are encouraged to explore ideas, ask questions and learn through discussions.
If an AI system is trained on specific data, it can inherit biases from these data.
The quality of decisions depends on the quality of the data that is often derived from human actions.
This does not mean that the technology or algorithm is specifically discriminated against.
Rather, it means that there is a present injustice in the given territory.
We need to address this injustice before we let AI learn from these biased data and make sure we have protective measures.
It is important to take diversity into account in all aspects of cooperation with AI.
If we did not notice it from the beginning, the decisions of AI would only deepen inequality.
As we have seen, AI can bring many benefits in solving problems that in many cases exceed human abilities.
But we also need to be aware of the limits of AI, which cannot be better in everything.
For example, AI is not capable of true creativity.
Its specialty is to create predictions and generate content based on previously seen data.
It can't go much further than that, always staying within the general limits of the original record.
Creativity is what makes people unique, and it is a force that cannot be replaced by technology.
Another area that is completely alien to AI is emotion and compassion.
AI can identify some emotions, and some of them can be imitated, but no robot can actually feel those emotions.
It is a unique human ability to interpret and engage with the emotions of others.
People are usually very good at perceiving emotions, even with distorted data.
With every technology, including AI, we need to think carefully about its impact on society.
Each example of AI application opens the ground for discussions and extended discussion about the pros and cons of use, its benefits and limitations, benefits and risks.
Living, learning and working in the digital world require awareness of their opportunities and risks derived from underlying mechanics.
At the same time, we must understand that technology itself is not good or bad, but an instrument that should be applied responsibly and fairly.
